figures.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison on bulk transcriptomics prediction. Best results are bolded, and the second best underlined.
Table 2: Performance comparison on spatial transcriptomics prediction. Best results are bolded, and the second best underlined.

Figure 1: Overview of the BiTro framework. There are four sequential phases: (a) WSI preprocessing: segment a WSI into cells by HoverNet or CellViT; (b) Cell feature extraction: extract cells’ visual features via Dinov3, capture cell phenotypes by K-means clustering, and add spatial coordinates; (c) Spatial enhancement: enhance cell features with both local and global spatial relations; (d) MIL and Bidirectional transfer learning: map cellular features to their bulk or spot-level transcriptomics by multiple instance learning. The learning process is enhanced by both levels of data via bidirectional transfer learning.
Figure 4: Visualization of BiTro’s inference on super-resolved spatial gene expression for gene EPCAM in the COAD sample TENX139 at both spot and cellular levels in the log1p space.

Table 8: Ablation study on spatial transcriptomics prediction. We compare our base and transfer models under three configurations: standard (Norm+Cluster), without clustering (w/o Clu.), without normalization (w/o Norm.), and without clustering and normalization (w/o Norm+Clu.). O. and G. represent Overall and Gene Pearson correlations. The best result for each dataset is bolded.

Figure 2: Model architecture. Inputs are cellular visual features H and their local spatial graph G. The GAT module and the Transformer encoder are to enhance H with spatial relations both locally and globally. The enhanced features Hcell are used to predict gene expression ŷ by multiple instance pooling.
